title: Testing
description: Unit tests, UI tests, testing coroutines, screenshot testing, and the testing pyramid
lesson:
Testing Android Apps

Android testing follows a pyramid: many unit tests, fewer integration tests, fewest UI tests.

  • Unit tests — Test individual classes (ViewModels, Repositories) with JUnit and fakes
  • Integration tests — Test component interactions
  • UI tests — Test user flows with Compose testing APIs

Key Tools

// Unit test with coroutines @Test fun loadUsers_emitsSuccess() = runTest { Dispatchers.setMain(UnconfinedTestDispatcher()) // ... test code } // Compose UI test composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Submit").performClick() composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Success").assertExists()

Google recommends fakes over mocks for most testing. Fakes have real logic, making tests more reliable.

The Testing Pyramid

/ UI Tests \ ~10% — Slow, flaky, high confidence / Integration \ ~20% — Medium speed, real components / Unit Tests \ ~70% — Fast, isolated, deterministic
Key insight: Unit tests are cheap and fast — write many. UI tests are expensive and flaky — write few but covering critical user journeys. Integration tests fill the gap, testing real component interactions without full UI.

Screenshot Testing

Catch visual regressions by comparing screenshots pixel-by-pixel:

ToolRuns onSpeedSetup
RoborazziJVM (Robolectric)Fast — no emulatorSimple Gradle plugin
PaparazziJVM (layoutlib)Fast — no emulatorSquare's library
Compose Preview ScreenshotJVMFastGoogle's official tool
// Roborazzi screenshot test @RunWith(RobolectricTestRunner::class) class ProfileScreenTest { @get:Rule val composeTestRule = createComposeRule() @Test fun profileScreen_default() { composeTestRule.setContent { ProfileScreen(user = testUser) } composeTestRule.onRoot().captureRoboImage() } }

Robolectric vs Instrumented Tests

RobolectricInstrumented
Runs on JVM — fast (seconds)Runs on device/emulator — slow (minutes)
Simulates Android frameworkReal Android framework
May have fidelity gaps100% fidelity
Great for: logic, simple UINeeded for: hardware, sensors, complex UI

Testing with Hilt

@HiltAndroidTest @UninstallModules(RepositoryModule::class) class SearchScreenTest { @get:Rule val hiltRule = HiltAndroidRule(this) @get:Rule val composeTestRule = createComposeRule() @BindValue val fakeRepo: UserRepository = FakeUserRepository() @Before fun setup() { hiltRule.inject() } @Test fun showsResults() { composeTestRule.setContent { SearchScreen() } composeTestRule.onNodeWithText("Results").assertExists() } }

questions.title: Compose UI Test
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to write a Compose UI test.
questions.explanation: createComposeRule() creates a test environment. onNodeWithText() finds by text. assertExists() verifies presence. onNode(hasText()) uses matchers. performClick() simulates user input.
hints: The test rule creates a Compose testing environment

questions.title: Testing Coroutines
questions.description: This ViewModel test is flaky — sometimes passes, sometimes fails. Fix the coroutine testing setup.

checks.message: Use runTest and set up a TestDispatcher

questions.explanation: Coroutines in viewModelScope use Dispatchers.Main, which doesn't exist in unit tests. Fix: Dispatchers.setMain(testDispatcher) replaces Main. runTest properly advances coroutine execution. Always reset in @After.
hints: viewModelScope uses Dispatchers.Main — does that exist in a JVM test?

questions.title: Test Doubles
questions.description: Which test double provides a simplified but working implementation?
options: Mock — records interactions
Stub — returns hardcoded values
Fake — has a working implementation (e.g., in-memory database)
Spy — wraps a real object
questions.explanation: A Fake is a simplified but functional implementation. Example: FakeUserRepository using a HashMap instead of a real database. Google recommends fakes over mocks for most Android testing.
hints: Which test double actually has working logic?

questions.title: ViewModel Unit Test
questions.description: Write a unit test for a ViewModel that tests the loading state flow: initial Loading → Success with data.
questions.explanation: Key: test dispatcher replaces Dispatchers.Main, fake repo provides data, runTest handles coroutines, assert against StateFlow value.
hints: You need three things: test dispatcher, fake repository, and runTest

questions.title: Turbine Flow Testing
questions.description: What does this test verify using the Turbine library?
options: It verifies that every keystroke triggers a search
It verifies that the search debounces — only the last query ("kotlin") produces results after 500ms
It verifies that the search filters out short queries
It verifies that search results are cached
questions.explanation: Turbine is a testing library for Kotlin Flows. .test { } collects emissions. Three queries are sent rapidly, then advanceTimeBy(500) simulates the debounce delay. Only one emission is expected — the results for "kotlin". This confirms the debounce works: intermediate queries are ignored, only the final one triggers a search after the delay.
hints: advanceTimeBy simulates time passing — why is that needed for debounce?
Only one awaitItem() is called — meaning only one emission is expected

questions.title: Fake Repository
questions.description: Write a FakeUserRepository that implements UserRepository interface. It should store users in a mutable list and support getAll(), insert(), and clear() operations.

checks.message: Store users in an in-memory list

questions.explanation: A fake uses real data structures (in-memory list) instead of a real database. toList() returns a defensive copy. Extra methods like setUsers() and clear() make test setup easy. This is simpler and more reliable than mocking.
hints: Use a MutableList as the backing store
Add helper methods for test setup convenience

questions.title: Testing Pyramid
questions.description: According to the testing pyramid, which type of test should make up the majority of your test suite?
options: UI / end-to-end tests — they give the most confidence
Integration tests — they balance speed and confidence
Unit tests — they are fast, isolated, and deterministic
Manual tests — automated tests can't catch everything
questions.explanation: The testing pyramid recommends ~70% unit tests, ~20% integration, ~10% UI tests. Unit tests are fast (milliseconds), run on JVM, and test logic in isolation. They're cheap to write and maintain. UI tests give high confidence but are slow and flaky. A healthy test suite has many fast unit tests and few targeted UI tests for critical flows.
hints: Which test type is fastest and cheapest to maintain?

questions.title: Screenshot Testing Tools
questions.description: Which screenshot testing tool runs entirely on the JVM (no emulator needed) and uses Robolectric's native rendering?
options: Espresso Screenshot
Roborazzi — JVM-based screenshot capture using Robolectric
Firebase Test Lab — cloud device screenshots
Layout Inspector — manual visual comparison
questions.explanation: Roborazzi captures screenshots on the JVM using Robolectric's rendering engine. No emulator or device needed — tests run in seconds. It compares against golden images and fails if there are visual differences beyond a threshold. Paparazzi (by Square) is similar but uses Android's layoutlib directly. Both are fast CI-friendly alternatives to device-based screenshot tests.
hints: The fastest screenshot tests don't need a device at all

questions.title: Robolectric vs Instrumented
questions.description: When should you prefer instrumented tests over Robolectric?
options: Always — Robolectric is outdated and unreliable
When you need 100% fidelity with real Android behavior, such as testing hardware sensors, complex animations, or multi-process scenarios
For all ViewModel tests to ensure accuracy
When testing pure Kotlin logic with no Android dependencies
questions.explanation: Robolectric simulates the Android framework on JVM — it's fast but may have fidelity gaps for advanced features. Use instrumented tests when you need the real Android framework: hardware sensors, camera, complex touch gestures, multi-process IPC, or behavior that differs across API levels. For ViewModels, Repositories, and simple UI, Robolectric is faster and sufficient.
hints: Robolectric simulates Android — some edge cases behave differently

questions.title: Hilt Test Setup
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to set up a Hilt-enabled test that replaces the real repository with a fake.

blanks.hint: Marks a test class for Hilt injection
Removes the real module so you can replace it
Binds this field directly into the Hilt graph

questions.explanation: @HiltAndroidTest enables Hilt in tests. @UninstallModules removes the production module that provides UserRepository. @BindValue injects the fake directly into the graph — no module needed. hiltRule.inject() triggers injection. This pattern lets you test with controlled data without mocking frameworks.
hints: Three annotations set up the replacement: test marker, uninstall, and bind
@BindValue is simpler than creating a test module

questions.title: Screenshot Test
questions.description: Write a Roborazzi screenshot test that captures the UserCard composable in both light and dark themes.
questions.explanation: Screenshot tests render a composable and capture an image. On first run, it creates golden images. On subsequent runs, it compares against goldens and fails if there's a visual difference. Testing both themes catches color/contrast regressions. captureRoboImage() is Roborazzi's API — Paparazzi uses paparazzi.snapshot { }.
hints: Wrap the composable in your theme with darkTheme parameter
captureRoboImage saves/compares against a golden image file
